---
title: Data Analytics Project
subtitle: "Final End of Year Project"
author:
  - name: Erin Mansfield 
    affiliation: SUNY Geneseo
    
  - name: Jack Mansfield 
    affiliation: SUNY Geneseo
    
date: last-modified

execute: 
  echo: true
  eval: true
  warning: false
  message: false
  fig-width: 10
  fig-height: 6

format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
    toc: true
    toc-title: "Contents"
    toc-expand: 3
    number-sections: true
    code-tools: true
    highlight-style: atom-one
---

```{r setup}
#| include: false
library(tidyverse)
library(skimr)
library(ggthemes)
library(hrbrthemes)
library(rmarkdown)
library(DT)

# Add other packages as needed.
# Examples:
# library(broom)
# library(stargazer)
# library(modelsummary)
# library(glmnet)
# library(ranger)
# library(xgboost)

# Feel free to revise this ggplot theme_set
theme_set(theme_ipsum()+
          theme(strip.background =element_rect(fill="lightgray"),
                axis.title.x = 
                  element_text(angle = 0,
                               size = rel(1.33),
                               margin = margin(10,0,0,0)),
                axis.title.y = 
                  element_text(angle = 90,
                               size = rel(1.33),
                               margin = margin(0,10,0,0))
                )
          )


# Feel free to use below global options for color-blind-friendly scales
# scale_colour_discrete <- function(...) scale_colour_viridis_d(...)
# scale_fill_discrete <- function(...) scale_fill_viridis_d(...)
```

# Introduction

The National Football League has changed multiple times over the past few years with their offensive strategy. From those changes, came questions on how the scoring patterns have been affected over time. As the NFL has evolved, analysts and fans have made claims that the NFL is turning into an offensive game. However, these claims are not necessary based on data analysis. 

This particular project looks at if scoring has truly increased from the years 2010-2025. The data identifies when, where, and how the scoring has varied across not just seasons, but weeks. 

You will see throughout this project that there is a clear data picture of how NFL scoring has evolved over time. It highlights the weeks and the seasons where the offensive performance may deviate from the past. 

# Background and Related Literature

The background comes from NFL scoring trends discussed by sports analysts. Analysts have claimed that the increase in offensive scoring to rule changes, quarterback development, and the rise in different offensive plays. Analysis done by ESPN and other sports data sites have shown short term spikes in scoring. However, looking at more long term data, there are more patterns that show a more consistent game pattern.

The project pulls the data from the website Kaggle. The data is 15 seasons from the NFL. There are cleaning procedures done, and shows a league wide view of scoring behavior throughout time. 

# Data

## Data Source

The dataset is from Kaggle with NFL game logs from the 2010-2025 seasons. The row represents an NFL game, with team names, scores, week, season, game date, and game status. 

## Unit of Observation

Each row is one NFL game. This is a perfect unit for scoring patterns analysts. 

## Key Variables

The key variables include: 
-HomeScore, AwayScore: raw points scored by each team
-total_points: sum of both teams' scores
-point_diff: absolute difference between scores 
-winner/loser: team outcome indicators 
-home_win: whether the home team won
-GameDate: cleaned and standardized date
-week_num: numeric week extracted from the "WEEEK X" label
-Season: year of the season

## Data Cleaning

Major cleaning steps: 
- Removing suffixes (st, nd, rd, th) from game dates
-Reconstructing full dates using the season year
-Converting dates
-Creating new scoring variables 
-Filtering
-Extracting numeric week values 
-Handling missing or inconsistent entries


# Empirical Strategy / Methods

The goal of the project is to describe NFL scoring and how it has changed from years 2010-2025. The project identifies patterns across the seasons. The empirical strategy is on grouped summaries and visual analytics.

The analysis is in four steps: 
1. Construct scoring variables: Computed total points, point differential, winner/loser indicators, and cleaned game dates. 
2. Restrict to regular season games: Preseason and postseason games follow different scoring dynamics, so I changed the sample to only included the games labeled "FINAL" and "WEEK".
3. Compute season-level and week level summaries: Calculated the average total points per season, average points per week, and distributions of point differentials. 
4. Use visualization as a primary analytical tool: Created an interactive plotly line chart, an aminated bar chart, additional static ggplots.


# Discussion and Implications

The analysis shows clear patterns in NFL scoring from 2010-2025. The first is the average points per game has a very upward trend over the period, including the early 2020s. This does align with the narratives that there is an increase in offensive efficiency, rule changes, and rise of more impressive offenses. 

The second pattern is that scoring is not clearly uniform across the season. In the early weeks, there is more low scoring, but in mid season it shows the highest scoring averages. The late season weeks have more high and low peaks. This can be explained through injuries, weather, and more playoff incentives. 

The third pattern is that the distribution of point differentials indicate that close games are common, however, high scoring blowouts are becoming more and more frequent. 

# Conclusion

The project shows a comprehensive look at NFL scoring and how it has evolved from 2010-2025. There is game level data with visualization driven analysis. The results show an upward trend with scoring, with variation across weeks and seasons. The animated and interactive visualizations show the offensive perfomrance, and an understanding of the league-wide scoring behavior. 

# References

1. Kaggle
2. ESPN
